headline: Vodafone Acquires Mannesmann for $180 Billion
subheadline: Largest corporate takeover in history reshapes global telecoms
body:
Vodafone AirTouch completed its hostile takeover of German industrial and telecoms giant Mannesmann AG for approximately $180 billion, marking the largest corporate acquisition in history at the time. The deal was finalised after a protracted battle in which Vodafone raised its offer multiple times to win over Mannesmann shareholders.

The transaction created the world's largest mobile telecommunications company, with operations spanning Europe, Asia, and the Americas. Vodafone gained access to Mannesmann's fast-growing Italian and German mobile networks, significantly strengthening its pan-European footprint.

The deal required approval from regulators across multiple jurisdictions, including the European Commission, which cleared it subject to the divestiture of certain overlapping assets. The transaction closed in February 2000 and remains a landmark in the history of global M&A.

headline: AOL Merges with Time Warner in $165 Billion Deal
subheadline: Internet giant and media conglomerate combine in ill-fated mega-merger
body:
America Online announced a merger with Time Warner in a stock-and-debt transaction valued at approximately $165 billion, combining the world's largest internet service provider with one of the biggest media and entertainment conglomerates. The deal was widely celebrated as the dawn of a new media era at the height of the dot-com boom.

The merged entity was to bring together AOL's 30 million subscribers with Time Warner's CNN, HBO, Warner Bros., and vast cable network infrastructure. Executives promised synergies from distributing Time Warner content through AOL's internet platform.

The deal is widely regarded as one of the worst corporate mergers in history. The combined company wrote down $99 billion in goodwill in 2002 as AOL's value collapsed. Time Warner eventually spun off AOL in 2009, and the two companies continued separately thereafter.

headline: Exxon Acquires Mobil for $81 Billion
subheadline: Oil majors reunite in historic deal creating world's largest publicly traded energy company
body:
Exxon Corporation completed its acquisition of Mobil Corporation for approximately $81 billion in stock, creating ExxonMobil — the largest publicly traded oil and gas company in the world. The deal reunited two of the original Standard Oil companies that had been broken up by antitrust regulators in 1911.

The merger was driven by plummeting oil prices in the late 1990s, which pressured companies to achieve greater scale and cost efficiencies. The combined entity promised $2.8 billion in annual cost savings through elimination of duplicated infrastructure, operations, and administrative functions.

The FTC approved the merger subject to significant divestitures, requiring the companies to sell over 2,400 gas stations, a refinery, and various pipeline interests. The deal closed in November 1999 and ExxonMobil remains one of the most profitable corporations in history.

headline: AB InBev Acquires SABMiller for $107 Billion
subheadline: World's two largest brewers combine to control nearly 30% of global beer volume
body:
Anheuser-Busch InBev completed the acquisition of SABMiller in October 2016 for approximately £79 billion ($107 billion), creating a brewing behemoth that controls nearly one-third of the world's beer by volume. The deal ranks among the largest consumer goods transactions in history.

To win regulatory approval across multiple continents, AB InBev was required to divest a substantial portion of SABMiller's portfolio. Most notably, it sold SABMiller's 58% stake in MillerCoors to Molson Coors for $12 billion, along with assets in Europe and Africa.

The combined company, which retained the AB InBev name, gained dominant positions across Latin America, Africa, and Asia, with brands including Budweiser, Corona, Stella Artois, Peroni, and Grolsch. The deal closed after more than a year of regulatory review spanning the EU, US, China, and South Africa.

headline: Disney Acquires 21st Century Fox for $71 Billion
subheadline: Entertainment giant secures Fox studios, National Geographic, and Hulu stake
body:
The Walt Disney Company completed its acquisition of 21st Century Fox's entertainment assets for approximately $71 billion in cash and stock, in one of the most significant media deals in Hollywood history. The deal gave Disney control of the Fox film and TV studios, FX Networks, National Geographic, Star India, and a majority stake in Hulu.

The acquisition was largely motivated by Disney's ambition to build a direct-to-consumer streaming business to compete with Netflix. The Fox content library, including franchises such as X-Men, Avatar, The Simpsons, and Alien, significantly bolstered Disney's content arsenal ahead of the Disney+ launch in November 2019.

The deal required Fox to divest its regional sports networks and other assets to satisfy DOJ concerns. Comcast made a competing all-cash bid for Fox in 2018 but Disney ultimately prevailed. The transaction closed in March 2019 after receiving regulatory clearance in the US, EU, and key international markets.

headline: Dell Acquires EMC for $67 Billion
subheadline: Largest technology acquisition in history creates enterprise IT powerhouse
body:
Dell Inc. completed its acquisition of EMC Corporation for approximately $67 billion in September 2016, the largest technology acquisition ever completed at the time. The deal combined Dell's PC and server hardware business with EMC's storage, cloud, and virtualisation portfolio, including its majority stake in VMware.

Michael Dell and private equity firm Silver Lake Partners structured the complex transaction using a combination of cash, debt, and a tracking stock tied to VMware's performance. The combined entity, renamed Dell Technologies, became one of the world's largest privately controlled technology companies.

The strategic rationale centred on offering enterprise customers a comprehensive portfolio spanning PCs, servers, storage, networking, and virtualisation under one roof. The deal was financed with approximately $49.5 billion in debt, making it one of the most leveraged buyouts in history. VMware remained publicly traded as a subsidiary.

headline: Microsoft Acquires Activision Blizzard for $69 Billion
subheadline: Gaming mega-deal cleared after 20-month regulatory battle across three continents
body:
Microsoft completed its $68.7 billion all-cash acquisition of Activision Blizzard in October 2023, the largest gaming deal in history and one of the most scrutinised transactions of the decade. The deal brought Call of Duty, World of Warcraft, Candy Crush, and Diablo under the Xbox Game Studios umbrella.

The acquisition faced fierce opposition from regulators worldwide. The FTC sought to block the deal in US federal court but failed. The UK's Competition and Markets Authority initially blocked the transaction before clearing a restructured version in which Microsoft agreed to divest Activision's cloud gaming rights to Ubisoft.

The deal positions Microsoft to accelerate its Game Pass subscription service and compete more aggressively in mobile gaming. Activision Blizzard's approximately 400 million monthly active users and deep game library represent a significant expansion of Microsoft's gaming footprint globally.

headline: Amazon Acquires MGM for $8.5 Billion
subheadline: E-commerce giant secures iconic Hollywood studio and James Bond franchise
body:
Amazon completed its $8.45 billion acquisition of MGM Holdings in March 2022, bringing the legendary Hollywood studio with its 4,000-film and 17,000-TV-episode library into the Amazon Prime Video ecosystem. The deal was Amazon's second-largest acquisition after its $13.7 billion purchase of Whole Foods.

MGM's library includes the James Bond franchise, Rocky, Creed, The Handmaid's Tale, and the Epix premium cable channel. Amazon Studios gains access to decades of intellectual property to feed its growing Prime Video and MGM+ streaming platforms.

The FTC considered challenging the deal but allowed it to proceed in March 2022 without conditions. Amazon has since integrated MGM content into Prime Video and rebranded the Epix channel as MGM+, using the studio's brand recognition to compete with Netflix and Disney+ for streaming subscribers.

headline: Pfizer Acquires Wyeth for $68 Billion
subheadline: Pharmaceutical giant diversifies pipeline with blockbuster vaccine and biologic assets
body:
Pfizer Inc. completed its acquisition of Wyeth for approximately $68 billion in October 2009, the largest pharmaceutical deal since the wave of mega-mergers that created Pfizer itself through its acquisitions of Warner-Lambert and Pharmacia. The transaction was driven by Pfizer's urgent need to offset revenue losses from impending patent expirations on blockbuster drugs including Lipitor.

Wyeth brought a diverse portfolio of vaccines, biologics, and consumer healthcare products, including Prevnar, one of the world's best-selling vaccines, and the Centrum vitamin brand. The deal significantly expanded Pfizer's biopharmaceuticals division at a time when the industry was shifting toward more complex biological medicines.

Pfizer financed the acquisition with a combination of cash and stock, raising approximately $22.5 billion in debt financing. The deal required divestitures in certain markets and closed after receiving regulatory approval from the FTC and the European Commission.

headline: Berkshire Hathaway Acquires BNSF Railway for $44 Billion
subheadline: Warren Buffett's biggest ever bet — an 'all-in wager on the economic future of the United States'
body:
Berkshire Hathaway completed its acquisition of Burlington Northern Santa Fe Corporation for approximately $44 billion in February 2010, the largest acquisition in Berkshire's history. Warren Buffett described the deal as an 'all-in wager on the economic future of the United States', signalling his long-term confidence in American infrastructure and commerce.

Berkshire already owned approximately 22% of BNSF prior to the acquisition and paid $100 per share in cash and stock for the remaining shares, representing a 31% premium to BNSF's pre-announcement price. The deal was financed through a combination of Berkshire cash and approximately $8 billion in debt.

BNSF operates one of North America's largest freight rail networks, spanning 32,500 miles across 28 states and two Canadian provinces. The railway transports coal, agricultural products, consumer goods, and industrial materials, making it a core artery of the US economy. BNSF has remained a major contributor to Berkshire's earnings in the years since.
